title: STEM Skills, Safety, and Investigation Habits
unitGoal: Students will practise asking testable questions, planning fair tests, using tools safely, recording data, and explaining conclusions with evidence.

lessons.title: Science as Careful Observation
lessons.learningGoal: Students will separate observations from guesses and describe details accurately.
successCriteria: I can tell the difference between an observation and an inference.
I can use specific details instead of vague descriptions.
I can notice when a claim needs more evidence.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise careful observation, inference, and evidence-based description.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that good science begins with careful observation. They practise telling what they can actually notice apart from what they think might be happening.
lessons.christianFocus: Science is one way to carefully study God's created world. Students should practise honesty, patience, and humility when they observe.
lessonContent: An observation is something you notice using your senses or a tool.
An inference is a careful idea based on observations, but it is not the same as proof.
Clear observations use exact details, measurements, and careful wording.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: Careful Observation Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise noticing details and separating observations from guesses.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: Science as Careful Observation Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: Careful Observation Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise noticing details and separating observations from guesses.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: observe before you explain
steps.text: Scientists do not begin by guessing wildly. They observe first. An observation tells what you can notice with your senses or tools. An inference is a careful idea about what the observation may mean.

steps.title: Observation or Inference?
steps.prompt: A student says, 'The liquid is clear and has small bubbles on the sides of the cup.' What is this?
steps.correctAnswer: An observation
steps.feedback: Correct. The student is describing what can be seen.
steps.hint: Look for wording that tells what is directly noticed.

steps.title: Careful Wording
steps.prompt: Which sentence is the best scientific observation?
choices: The powder looks like salt because it is white.
The powder is white and has small grains.
The powder is probably safe because it looks clean.
steps.correctAnswer: The powder is white and has small grains.
steps.feedback: Yes. It describes what is noticed without jumping ahead.
steps.hint: A good observation avoids guessing what the object is.

steps.title: Tools Help
steps.prompt: Why might a hand lens improve an observation?
choices: It can show details that are hard to see with eyes alone.
It proves the first guess was correct.
It changes the object so the answer is easier.
steps.correctAnswer: It can show details that are hard to see with eyes alone.
steps.feedback: Correct. Tools can help us notice more accurate details.
steps.hint: Think about what the tool helps you see.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A careful observation should include only what a person hopes will happen.
steps.correctAnswer: False
steps.feedback: Correct. Observations should report what is noticed, not what someone wants to be true.
steps.hint: Honest science does not change details to match a wish.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You practised making careful observations.

steps.title: Inference and Evidence
steps.prompt: Use observations to support ideas
steps.text: An inference can be useful, but it needs evidence. If two explanations could fit the same observation, a scientist should keep investigating instead of pretending the answer is already certain.

steps.title: Inference Check
steps.prompt: A plant's leaves are drooping. Which is the best inference?
choices: The plant may need water, but more checking is needed.
The plant is trying to look sad.
steps.correctAnswer: The plant may need water, but more checking is needed.
steps.feedback: Correct. It is a reasonable idea, but it still needs evidence.
steps.hint: Choose the answer that is careful, not overconfident.

steps.title: Evidence Needed
steps.prompt: Which observation would best help check whether a ramp is steeper than another ramp?
choices: Measure each ramp's height and length.
Notice which ramp looks more exciting.
Ask which ramp a friend likes better.
steps.correctAnswer: Measure each ramp's height and length.
steps.feedback: Yes. Measurements give better evidence than opinions.
steps.hint: A fair comparison needs measurable details.

steps.title: Not Enough Evidence
steps.prompt: A student sees one bird near a feeder and says, 'All birds like this seed best.' What is the main problem?
choices: One observation is not enough to make such a broad claim.
Seed choice is always impossible to study.
steps.correctAnswer: One observation is not enough to make such a broad claim.
steps.feedback: Correct. A big claim needs stronger evidence.
steps.hint: Think about whether the evidence is enough for the claim.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: It is honest to say 'I need more evidence' when the observations are not enough.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Careful scientists admit when more evidence is needed.
steps.hint: Good science includes humility.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised using evidence carefully.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is an observation?
choices: The rock is rough and grey.
The rock must be very old.
The rock is probably from a river.
questions.correctAnswer: The rock is rough and grey.
questions.reviewNote: An observation describes what can be noticed.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is an inference?
choices: The drink may have been heated.
questions.correctAnswer: The drink may have been heated.
questions.reviewNote: An inference is an idea based on observations.

questions.prompt: Why do scientists often use tools when observing?
choices: Tools can make observations more accurate.
Tools replace the need for careful thinking.
Tools make every inference automatically true.
questions.correctAnswer: Tools can make observations more accurate.
questions.reviewNote: Tools can extend our senses and improve accuracy.

questions.prompt: Which claim is the most careful?
choices: The plant may need water because the soil feels dry.
The plant is ruined because one leaf is drooping.
The plant is happy because it is green.
questions.correctAnswer: The plant may need water because the soil feels dry.
questions.reviewNote: Careful claims connect evidence to a reasonable idea.

questions.prompt: What should a student do if two explanations fit the same observation?
choices: Keep investigating and gather more evidence.
Choose the explanation that sounds easiest.
questions.correctAnswer: Keep investigating and gather more evidence.
questions.reviewNote: More evidence can help choose between possible explanations.

questions.prompt: An inference can be useful, but it should not be treated as proof without evidence.
questions.reviewNote: Inferences need evidence.

questions.prompt: Good science allows a student to change observations so they fit the expected answer.
questions.reviewNote: Observations should be reported honestly.

questions.prompt: A student says, 'The metal spoon is colder than the wooden spoon.' What would make this observation stronger?
choices: Measuring each spoon's temperature with a thermometer
Choosing the spoon that looks shinier
Asking which spoon feels nicer to hold
questions.correctAnswer: Measuring each spoon's temperature with a thermometer
questions.reviewNote: A measurement gives stronger evidence than a quick feeling.

lessons.title: Asking Testable Questions
lessons.learningGoal: Students will turn broad science questions into questions that can be investigated.
successCriteria: I can recognize a testable question.
I can improve a question so it can be investigated.
I can avoid questions that only ask for opinions.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise choosing and improving questions that can be investigated with evidence.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that a testable question can be answered by collecting observations or measurements, not just by giving an opinion.
lessons.christianFocus: Wonder is a good starting point, but students should learn to ask honest questions that can be explored carefully.
lessonContent: A testable question can be investigated using observations, measurements, or a fair comparison.
Opinion questions can be interesting, but they are not the same as testable science questions.
Strong questions are clear enough that another person could understand what to test.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: Testable Questions Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise turning wonder questions into questions you can investigate.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: Asking Testable Questions Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.
quizPlan.focus: Testable questions, opinion questions, and improved investigation questions

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: Testable Questions Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise turning wonder questions into questions you can investigate.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: questions that can be tested
steps.text: A science question is stronger when it can be investigated with observations or measurements. 'Which paper towel absorbs more water?' can be tested. 'Which paper towel is best?' is too vague unless you explain what best means.

steps.title: Choose the Testable Question
steps.prompt: Which question could be investigated with measurements?
choices: Which cup keeps water warm the longest?
Which cup would my friend choose?
steps.correctAnswer: Which cup keeps water warm the longest?
steps.feedback: Correct. You could measure temperature over time.
steps.hint: Look for something that can be measured.

steps.title: Opinion or Test?
steps.prompt: Which question is mostly asking for an opinion?
choices: Which magnet can pick up the most paper clips?
steps.correctAnswer: Which magnet looks coolest?
steps.feedback: Yes. 'Coolest' depends on personal opinion unless it is defined.
steps.hint: Opinion words often need clearer meanings.

steps.title: Clear Comparison
steps.prompt: Which question gives the clearest comparison?
choices: Does sunlight affect plant growth over two weeks?
Do plants grow in a special way?
steps.correctAnswer: Does sunlight affect plant growth over two weeks?
steps.feedback: Correct. It names what is being studied and gives a time frame.
steps.hint: A clear question often names what will be changed or measured.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A testable question should be clear enough that someone could plan what evidence to collect.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. A testable question guides the investigation.
steps.hint: Think about whether the question points to evidence.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You can spot questions that are easier to investigate.

steps.title: Make Questions Stronger
steps.prompt: Narrow the question
steps.text: Many weak questions can be improved. Add what will be compared, what will be measured, and how long the test will last. This makes the question easier to investigate fairly.

steps.title: Make It Testable
steps.prompt: How could you improve 'Which soil is best?'
choices: Which soil helps bean plants grow tallest in three weeks?
Which soil has the nicest colour?
Which soil would everyone like most?
steps.correctAnswer: Which soil helps bean plants grow tallest in three weeks?
steps.feedback: Correct. It says what will be measured and when.
steps.hint: A stronger question explains what 'best' means.

steps.title: Too Broad
steps.prompt: Why is 'How does weather affect people?' too broad for a simple class investigation?
choices: It does not say which weather effect or what will be measured.
steps.correctAnswer: It does not say which weather effect or what will be measured.
steps.feedback: Yes. It needs to be narrowed.
steps.hint: Broad questions usually need more focus.

steps.title: Better Question
steps.prompt: Which version is best for a fair investigation?
choices: Which ball bounces highest when dropped from one metre?
Which ball is the most fun?
steps.correctAnswer: Which ball bounces highest when dropped from one metre?
steps.feedback: Correct. It can be tested and measured.
steps.hint: Find the version with a clear action and measurement.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: Adding a measurement can often make a science question easier to investigate.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Measurements help turn ideas into evidence.
steps.hint: Think of height, time, distance, mass, or temperature.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised improving science questions.

questions.prompt: Which question is most testable?
questions.reviewNote: Distance can be measured.

questions.prompt: Which question mostly asks for an opinion?
choices: Which design holds the most weight?
Which design lasts longest in water?
questions.reviewNote: Beauty is an opinion unless it is clearly defined.

questions.prompt: What makes 'Which ice cube melts fastest in different places?' testable?
choices: The melting time can be measured.
The answer depends only on what someone likes.
questions.correctAnswer: The melting time can be measured.
questions.reviewNote: A measurable outcome makes it testable.

questions.prompt: Which version best improves 'Do seeds grow well?'
choices: Which seed sprouts first in damp paper towel?
Do seeds know when to grow?
questions.correctAnswer: Which seed sprouts first in damp paper towel?
questions.reviewNote: It names what will be measured.

questions.prompt: A strong testable question usually helps a student decide...
questions.reviewNote: The question should guide the investigation.

questions.prompt: A question can be interesting but still not be testable in a science investigation.
questions.reviewNote: Some questions are better for discussion or research.

questions.prompt: The question 'Which towel absorbs the most water?' is more testable than 'Which towel is best?'
questions.reviewNote: It defines what is being compared.

questions.prompt: Which detail would make a ramp question easier to test?
choices: Measure how far the toy car travels.
Ask which ramp looks more professional.
questions.correctAnswer: Measure how far the toy car travels.
questions.reviewNote: A distance measurement gives evidence.

lessons.title: Fair Tests and Variables
lessons.learningGoal: Students will identify changed, measured, and controlled variables in a fair test.
successCriteria: I can identify what is changed in a fair test.
I can identify what is measured.
I can explain why other conditions should stay the same.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise planning fair tests by changing one thing, measuring one result, and keeping other conditions the same.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that a fair test changes one main thing at a time so the evidence is easier to understand.
lessons.christianFocus: Fair testing encourages honesty and carefulness. Students should not design a test to force the answer they already want.
lessonContent: The changed variable is the one thing you choose to change.
The measured variable is the result you observe or measure.
Controlled variables are conditions kept the same so the test stays fair.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: Fair Test Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise spotting variables and keeping investigations fair.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: Fair Tests and Variables Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.
quizPlan.focus: Changed variables, measured variables, controlled variables, and fair comparisons

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: Fair Test Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise spotting variables and keeping investigations fair.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: change one thing
steps.text: In a fair test, you change one main thing and measure what happens. If too many things change at once, it becomes hard to know what caused the result.

steps.title: Changed Variable
steps.prompt: A student tests whether ramp height affects how far a toy car travels. What is the changed variable?
steps.correctAnswer: The ramp height
steps.feedback: Correct. Ramp height is the thing being changed on purpose.
steps.hint: Look for what the student changes.

steps.title: Measured Result
steps.prompt: In the ramp test, what should the student measure?
steps.correctAnswer: How far the car travels
steps.feedback: Yes. The distance is the result of the test.
steps.hint: Look for the outcome.

steps.title: Controlled Variable
steps.prompt: Which condition should stay the same in a fair ramp test?
choices: Use the same toy car each time.
Change the car and ramp each time.
Let each student choose any starting place.
steps.correctAnswer: Use the same toy car each time.
steps.feedback: Correct. Keeping the same car makes the comparison fairer.
steps.hint: A controlled variable stays the same.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: Changing one main thing at a time makes it easier to understand the results.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. That is the point of a fair test.
steps.hint: Too many changes make the evidence confusing.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You can identify variables in a test.

steps.title: Watch for Hidden Changes
steps.prompt: Small changes can matter
steps.text: Sometimes a test seems fair, but hidden changes make it weaker. A different amount of water, a different measuring tool, or a different starting point can change the result.

steps.title: Hidden Problem
steps.prompt: Two plants are tested with different amounts of light. One also gets more water. Why is this a problem?
choices: The test changed light and water, so the result is harder to explain.
Plants cannot be compared in science.
steps.correctAnswer: The test changed light and water, so the result is harder to explain.
steps.feedback: Correct. More than one important thing changed.
steps.hint: Ask whether only one main thing changed.

steps.title: Fairer Plan
steps.prompt: Which plan is fairest for testing which paper towel absorbs more water?
choices: Use the same amount of water and the same towel size.
Use more water on the thicker towel.
Let each towel soak for a different length of time.
steps.correctAnswer: Use the same amount of water and the same towel size.
steps.feedback: Yes. The towels can be compared more fairly.
steps.hint: Keep conditions the same except the towel type.

steps.title: Repeat Trials
steps.prompt: Why might a student repeat the same test several times?
choices: To check whether the result is consistent
To make the test less fair
steps.correctAnswer: To check whether the result is consistent
steps.feedback: Correct. Repeating trials can make evidence stronger.
steps.hint: Think about whether one try is always enough.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: If a test is unfair, the data may still be written down, but the conclusion should be careful.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Weak data needs careful explanation.
steps.hint: Being honest about limits matters.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised planning fair tests.

questions.prompt: In a test about whether ball material affects bounce height, what is the changed variable?
questions.reviewNote: The changed variable is what is changed on purpose.

questions.prompt: In that same test, what is the measured variable?
questions.reviewNote: The measured variable is the result.

questions.prompt: Which condition should stay the same in a fair bounce test?
questions.reviewNote: The drop height should be controlled.

questions.prompt: Why should a fair test usually change only one main thing?
choices: So the result is easier to explain
So the test becomes impossible to repeat
questions.correctAnswer: So the result is easier to explain
questions.reviewNote: One main change helps connect cause and result.

questions.prompt: A student tests plant growth, changing both soil type and water amount. What is the concern?
choices: It will be hard to know which change affected growth.
Soil and water cannot be studied together in any way.
The plants will all grow exactly the same.
questions.correctAnswer: It will be hard to know which change affected growth.
questions.reviewNote: Two changed variables make the test confusing.

questions.prompt: Controlled variables are the things kept the same in a fair test.
questions.reviewNote: Controlled variables help keep the test fair.

questions.prompt: A fair test should avoid recording measurements because they make results less clear.
questions.reviewNote: Measurements usually make results clearer.

questions.prompt: Repeating a test can help students...
choices: remove the need for a question
questions.reviewNote: Repeated trials can strengthen evidence.

lessons.title: Measuring and Recording Data
lessons.learningGoal: Students will choose suitable tools and record data clearly in tables or charts.
successCriteria: I can choose a tool that matches what I need to measure.
I can use units correctly.
I can organize data so another person can understand it.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise choosing tools, units, tables, and charts for clear science data.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that data is more useful when it is measured carefully and recorded in an organized way.
lessons.christianFocus: Careful records show respect for truth. Students should record what happened, not what they hoped would happen.
lessonContent: Data is information collected during an investigation.
Measurements need suitable tools and correct units.
Tables and graphs help people notice patterns in data.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: Data Recording Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise choosing tools, units, and clear ways to record science data.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: Measuring and Recording Data Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.
quizPlan.focus: Measurement tools, units, tables, graphs, and clear records

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: Data Recording Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise choosing tools, units, and clear ways to record science data.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: measure carefully
steps.text: A measurement is only useful if it matches the question. Length might be measured in centimetres. Temperature might be measured in degrees Celsius. Time might be measured in seconds or minutes.

steps.title: Choose the Tool
steps.prompt: Which tool would best measure how far a toy car travels?
steps.correctAnswer: Metre stick or measuring tape
steps.feedback: Correct. Distance needs a length tool.
steps.hint: Think about what is being measured.

steps.title: Choose the Unit
steps.prompt: Which unit would best record the temperature of water?
steps.correctAnswer: Degrees Celsius
steps.feedback: Yes. Temperature is measured in degrees Celsius.
steps.hint: Match the unit to the kind of measurement.

steps.title: Estimate First
steps.prompt: Why can estimating before measuring be useful?
choices: It helps you notice if the measurement seems unreasonable.
It replaces the need to measure.
steps.correctAnswer: It helps you notice if the measurement seems unreasonable.
steps.feedback: Correct. Estimating can help catch mistakes.
steps.hint: An estimate is not exact, but it can be helpful.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A measurement without a unit can be confusing.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. '12' could mean many different things without a unit.
steps.hint: Units tell what kind of amount was measured.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You practised matching tools and units.

steps.title: Record So Others Understand
steps.prompt: Use tables and graphs clearly
steps.text: A good data table has labels. It shows what was tested and what was measured. A graph can make patterns easier to notice, but the graph still needs a clear title and labels.

steps.title: Table Labels
steps.prompt: Why should a data table have headings?
choices: Headings explain what each column means.
Headings make the numbers less important.
Headings replace the need for units.
steps.correctAnswer: Headings explain what each column means.
steps.feedback: Correct. Headings make data easier to understand.
steps.hint: A table should be clear to someone else.

steps.title: Graph Choice
steps.prompt: Which graph would work well for comparing the bounce heights of three balls?
steps.correctAnswer: Bar graph
steps.feedback: Yes. A bar graph is useful for comparing categories.
steps.hint: Think about comparing separate items.

steps.title: Missing Information
steps.prompt: A table says 'Trial 1: 24, Trial 2: 26.' What is missing?
choices: The unit or what was measured
steps.correctAnswer: The unit or what was measured
steps.feedback: Correct. The numbers need labels and units.
steps.hint: Numbers alone are often not enough.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: Changing data after a test to make it look better is a poor scientific habit.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Data should be honest.
steps.hint: Science records should tell what happened.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised recording data clearly.

questions.prompt: Which tool would best measure mass?
questions.reviewNote: A balance scale measures mass.

questions.prompt: Which unit matches length?
questions.reviewNote: Centimetres measure length.

questions.prompt: Which tool would best measure water temperature?
questions.reviewNote: A thermometer measures temperature.

questions.prompt: Why should a data table include units?
choices: So the data cannot be compared
questions.reviewNote: Units explain what the numbers mean.

questions.prompt: Which display is useful for comparing categories?
questions.reviewNote: Bar graphs compare categories clearly.

questions.prompt: Data should be recorded honestly, even when it is different from the expected result.
questions.reviewNote: Unexpected results should still be recorded.

questions.prompt: The number 15 is always clear enough without a label or unit.
questions.reviewNote: A number needs context.

questions.prompt: Why might a student estimate before measuring?
choices: To notice if a measurement seems far off
To make the answer whatever they want
questions.correctAnswer: To notice if a measurement seems far off
questions.reviewNote: Estimates can help catch mistakes.

lessons.title: Evidence and Conclusions
lessons.learningGoal: Students will use evidence to support conclusions and describe limits in an investigation.
successCriteria: I can connect a conclusion to data.
I can recognize when evidence is weak.
I can explain a possible source of error.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise making conclusions that fit the evidence and noticing limits in data.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that conclusions should be based on evidence, not on guesses, preferences, or pressure to get a certain answer.
lessons.christianFocus: Truthfulness matters. Students should be willing to follow evidence even when the result is surprising.
lessonContent: A conclusion explains what the evidence shows.
A strong conclusion names the data or pattern that supports it.
A careful scientist can also name possible errors or limits.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: Evidence and Conclusions Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise choosing conclusions that match the evidence.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: Evidence and Conclusions Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.
quizPlan.focus: Claims, evidence, conclusions, patterns, and sources of error

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: Evidence and Conclusions Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise choosing conclusions that match the evidence.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: conclusions need evidence
steps.text: A conclusion should be connected to data. If a student says, 'The tall ramp made the car go farther,' the data should show that the car travelled farther from the tall ramp.

steps.title: Best Conclusion
steps.prompt: A car rolled 45 cm from a low ramp and 92 cm from a high ramp. Which conclusion fits best?
choices: The higher ramp made the car travel farther in this test.
The low ramp is always better.
The car was impossible to measure.
steps.correctAnswer: The higher ramp made the car travel farther in this test.
steps.feedback: Correct. That conclusion matches the data.
steps.hint: Choose the answer that stays close to the evidence.

steps.title: Evidence Support
steps.prompt: Which statement gives evidence?
choices: The plant grew 6 cm more in sunlight than in the cupboard.
The plant probably liked the sunny spot.
The sunny plant looked happier to me.
steps.correctAnswer: The plant grew 6 cm more in sunlight than in the cupboard.
steps.feedback: Yes. It gives a measurement.
steps.hint: Evidence often uses observations or measurements.

steps.title: Too Strong
steps.prompt: One test shows that one paper towel held more water. Which conclusion is too strong?
choices: This towel held more water in our test.
This brand is always the best for every spill.
More trials would make the evidence stronger.
steps.correctAnswer: This brand is always the best for every spill.
steps.feedback: Correct. That claim goes beyond the evidence.
steps.hint: Watch for words like always or every.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A conclusion should fit the evidence collected during the investigation.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Evidence and conclusion should match.
steps.hint: Conclusions should not float away from the data.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You practised matching conclusions to evidence.

steps.title: Careful Conclusions
steps.prompt: Name limits honestly
steps.text: Sometimes evidence is helpful but not complete. A careful conclusion can say what the data suggests and also name a limit, such as a small number of trials or a possible measuring mistake.

steps.title: Source of Error
steps.prompt: Which could be a source of error in a plant-height test?
choices: Measuring from a different starting point each time
steps.correctAnswer: Measuring from a different starting point each time
steps.feedback: Correct. Inconsistent measuring can affect the data.
steps.hint: A source of error can make data less accurate.

steps.title: Careful Language
steps.prompt: Which conclusion uses careful language?
choices: Our data suggests the thicker string held more weight.
The thicker string will never break.
The thinner string is useless for everything.
steps.correctAnswer: Our data suggests the thicker string held more weight.
steps.feedback: Yes. It uses the evidence without exaggerating.
steps.hint: Careful conclusions avoid overclaiming.

steps.title: Weak Evidence
steps.prompt: Why is one trial often weaker than several trials?
choices: One trial could be affected by a mistake or unusual result.
One trial always proves the opposite.
steps.correctAnswer: One trial could be affected by a mistake or unusual result.
steps.feedback: Correct. More trials can make a pattern clearer.
steps.hint: Think about mistakes and unusual results.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A student should hide possible errors so the conclusion sounds stronger.
steps.correctAnswer: False
steps.feedback: Correct. Honest science names possible errors.
steps.hint: Strong work can admit limits.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised writing careful conclusions.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion best fits data showing Trial A took 12 s and Trial B took 20 s?
choices: Trial A was faster in this test.
Trial B was always worse in every way.
questions.correctAnswer: Trial A was faster in this test.
questions.reviewNote: The conclusion should match the measured time.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses evidence?
choices: The bridge held 800 g before bending.
questions.correctAnswer: The bridge held 800 g before bending.
questions.reviewNote: A measurement can support a claim.

questions.prompt: Which phrase often makes a conclusion too strong?
questions.reviewNote: Always often goes beyond limited evidence.

questions.prompt: Which is a possible source of error?
questions.reviewNote: Timing mistakes can affect data.

questions.prompt: Why can repeated trials improve an investigation?
choices: They can show whether results are consistent.
They remove the need to record data.
They make every answer a guess.
questions.correctAnswer: They can show whether results are consistent.
questions.reviewNote: Repeated trials can strengthen evidence.

questions.prompt: A conclusion should be based on data, not only on what the student wanted to happen.
questions.reviewNote: A conclusion should follow the evidence.

questions.prompt: Naming a possible source of error can make a science explanation more honest.
questions.reviewNote: Honest limits help others understand the evidence.

questions.prompt: Which wording is best for limited data?
choices: No one should test this again because...
questions.reviewNote: This wording fits evidence that may still be limited.

lessons.title: The STEM Design Process
lessons.learningGoal: Students will use a design process to solve problems, test ideas, and improve solutions.
successCriteria: I can describe steps in the STEM design process.
I can explain why testing and improving matter.
I can connect design choices to a problem and criteria.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise the STEM design process: identify, imagine, plan, create, test, improve, and communicate.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that STEM design is not simply building. A good design responds to a problem, criteria, constraints, test results, and improvements.
lessons.christianFocus: Design work can serve others. Students should value careful work, creativity, honesty, and stewardship of materials.
lessonContent: Criteria describe what a successful design should do.
Constraints are limits such as time, materials, size, or cost.
A prototype is a first model that can be tested and improved.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: STEM Design Process Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise planning, testing, and improving a design solution.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: The STEM Design Process Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.
quizPlan.focus: Criteria, constraints, prototypes, testing, improvement, and communication

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: STEM Design Process Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise planning, testing, and improving a design solution.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: design solves a problem
steps.text: A STEM design challenge begins with a problem. The design also has criteria, which say what success looks like, and constraints, which are limits you must work within.

steps.title: Design Problem
steps.prompt: Which statement is a clear design problem?
choices: Build a paper bridge that can hold 500 g using only two sheets of paper.
Use supplies until the time is gone.
steps.correctAnswer: Build a paper bridge that can hold 500 g using only two sheets of paper.
steps.feedback: Correct. It gives a problem, goal, and limit.
steps.hint: A clear design problem includes what the design should do.

steps.title: Criteria
steps.prompt: In a bridge challenge, which is a criterion?
choices: The bridge must hold at least 500 g.
Only two sheets of paper may be used.
The class period ends in 30 minutes.
steps.correctAnswer: The bridge must hold at least 500 g.
steps.feedback: Yes. That tells what success should look like.
steps.hint: Criteria describe success.

steps.title: Constraint
steps.prompt: Which is a constraint?
choices: Only tape and paper may be used.
The design should be improved after testing.
steps.correctAnswer: Only tape and paper may be used.
steps.feedback: Correct. A constraint is a limit.
steps.hint: Look for a limit on materials, time, size, or cost.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A design plan can help a team avoid wasting materials.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Planning supports careful material use.
steps.hint: Planning before building is part of stewardship.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You practised planning a design.

steps.title: Improve with Evidence
steps.prompt: Testing is not failure
steps.text: A prototype is a first model. Testing a prototype helps you learn what works and what needs to change. Improving a design is a normal and important part of STEM.

steps.title: Prototype
steps.prompt: What is a prototype?
choices: A model built to test and improve an idea
A final answer that cannot be changed
A drawing that replaces all testing
steps.correctAnswer: A model built to test and improve an idea
steps.feedback: Correct. Prototypes are made for testing and learning.
steps.hint: Think of a first model.

steps.title: Use the Test
steps.prompt: A paper tower falls when weight is added. What should the team do next?
choices: Use the test results to improve the base or supports.
Throw away all data and start guessing.
steps.correctAnswer: Use the test results to improve the base or supports.
steps.feedback: Yes. Testing helps guide improvements.
steps.hint: A failed test can still teach something.

steps.title: Communicate
steps.prompt: Why should a team explain both what worked and what did not work?
choices: It helps others understand the design choices and evidence.
It makes the design look weaker automatically.
It replaces the need to test.
steps.correctAnswer: It helps others understand the design choices and evidence.
steps.feedback: Correct. Communication should be honest and useful.
steps.hint: STEM includes explaining.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: Improving a design after testing means the first design was useless.
steps.correctAnswer: False
steps.feedback: Correct. The first design can teach what to improve.
steps.hint: Testing and improving are normal.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised the STEM design process.

questions.prompt: Which design problem is clearest?
choices: Create a container that keeps an ice cube from melting quickly for 20 minutes.
questions.correctAnswer: Create a container that keeps an ice cube from melting quickly for 20 minutes.
questions.reviewNote: It gives a goal and time frame.

questions.prompt: Criteria tell a team...
choices: what a successful design should do
questions.correctAnswer: what a successful design should do
questions.reviewNote: Criteria describe success.

questions.prompt: Which is a constraint?
choices: The tower must use only 20 straws.
The tower should stand by itself.
questions.correctAnswer: The tower must use only 20 straws.
questions.reviewNote: A material limit is a constraint.

questions.prompt: A prototype is...
choices: a test model of a design idea
a final design that cannot change
questions.correctAnswer: a test model of a design idea
questions.reviewNote: A prototype is made to test and improve.

questions.prompt: What should guide design improvements?
choices: The choice that uses the most supplies
questions.reviewNote: Evidence should guide improvements.

questions.prompt: A design that needs improvement can still be part of good STEM work.
questions.reviewNote: Improvement is part of design.

questions.prompt: Communication in STEM should include evidence, not only a finished product.
questions.reviewNote: Explaining evidence is part of STEM.

questions.prompt: Which habit shows responsible design work?
choices: Use extra materials because they are nearby.
Hide test results that do not look good.
questions.reviewNote: Planning helps use materials wisely.

lessons.title: Safety and Research Habits
lessons.learningGoal: Students will identify safe investigation habits and reliable research habits.
successCriteria: I can choose safe actions during an investigation.
I can recognize why some sources are more reliable than others.
I can explain why teamwork and honesty matter in science.
lessons.teacherSummary: Students practise safety, reliable research, source checking, credit, and respectful teamwork.
lessons.teacherOverview: Students learn that good science includes safe procedures, trustworthy information, respectful collaboration, and honest credit for ideas.
lessons.christianFocus: Students should care for people, tell the truth, and give credit when using another person's work.
lessonContent: Safety rules protect people and materials.
Reliable sources should be checked for accuracy, expertise, and purpose.
Research notes should use your own words and give credit for information.
practiceIdeas: Share the student mission for practice stars.
Use the lesson quiz as the scored check.
Review missed ideas before assigning the next STEM lesson.

studentActivityPlan.title: Safety and Research Mission
studentActivityPlan.description: Practise safe investigation choices and wise research habits.
studentActivityPlan.reward: 10 practice stars after each completed level

quizPlan.title: Safety and Research Habits Quiz
quizPlan.format: Multiple choice and true/false questions with one score at the end.
quizPlan.focus: Safety, reliable sources, digital research, teamwork, and credit

lessons.assessmentPlan: Use the activity to build confidence and the quiz to check independent understanding.

studentActivity.title: Safety and Research Mission
studentActivity.mission: Practise safe investigation choices and wise research habits.

steps.title: Before You Begin
steps.prompt: Get ready: safety protects people
steps.text: Science activities should be safe and respectful. Read instructions before starting, use tools the right way, and ask an adult if something is unclear or unsafe.

steps.title: Unknown Substance
steps.prompt: What should a student do before touching or smelling an unknown substance?
choices: Ask the teacher or trusted adult for instructions.
Smell it closely to identify it.
Taste a tiny bit if it looks familiar.
steps.correctAnswer: Ask the teacher or trusted adult for instructions.
steps.feedback: Correct. Unknown substances need adult direction.
steps.hint: Safety comes before curiosity.

steps.title: Tool Safety
steps.prompt: Which action shows safe tool use?
choices: Carry scissors closed and pointed down.
Use a tool without reading directions.
steps.correctAnswer: Carry scissors closed and pointed down.
steps.feedback: Yes. Tools should be handled carefully.
steps.hint: Choose the action that protects people.

steps.title: Spill Response
steps.prompt: What should a student do after a spill during an investigation?
choices: Tell the teacher and follow cleanup instructions.
Hide it so no one worries.
Keep working and step around it.
steps.correctAnswer: Tell the teacher and follow cleanup instructions.
steps.feedback: Correct. Spills should be handled safely.
steps.hint: A spill can affect safety and data.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: Safety rules are only needed when an investigation seems dangerous.
steps.correctAnswer: False
steps.feedback: Correct. Safety habits matter in every investigation.
steps.hint: Even simple activities need care.

steps.title: Level 1 Complete
steps.prompt: You practised safe investigation choices.

steps.title: Check the Source
steps.prompt: Not every source is equally reliable
steps.text: Research is stronger when sources are reliable. Check who made the source, why it was made, whether it is current enough, and whether other trustworthy sources agree.

steps.title: Reliable Source
steps.prompt: Which source is likely strongest for learning about weather data?
choices: A weather agency page with recent measurements
A random comment that says 'storms are cool'
A drawing of a cloud with no explanation
steps.correctAnswer: A weather agency page with recent measurements
steps.feedback: Correct. It gives recent data from a relevant source.
steps.hint: Look for expertise and evidence.

steps.title: Credit
steps.prompt: Why should a student give credit when using information from a source?
choices: It shows honesty and helps others find the source.
It makes the project longer for no reason.
It means the student did not learn anything.
steps.correctAnswer: It shows honesty and helps others find the source.
steps.feedback: Yes. Credit is part of honest research.
steps.hint: Using someone else's work requires honesty.

steps.title: Teamwork
steps.prompt: Which teamwork habit is best during a STEM task?
choices: Listen to evidence and share jobs fairly.
steps.correctAnswer: Listen to evidence and share jobs fairly.
steps.feedback: Correct. STEM teamwork should be respectful and useful.
steps.hint: Good teamwork helps the investigation.

steps.title: True or False
steps.prompt: A website can look nice and still need to be checked for reliability.
steps.correctAnswer: True
steps.feedback: Correct. Appearance is not the same as reliability.
steps.hint: Reliable sources need more than a nice design.

steps.title: Mission Complete
steps.prompt: You practised safe and wise research habits.

questions.prompt: What should a student do with an unknown substance?
choices: Ask for adult instructions before touching or smelling it.
Taste it only if it looks safe.
Mix it with another substance to see what happens.
questions.correctAnswer: Ask for adult instructions before touching or smelling it.
questions.reviewNote: Unknown substances require safety care.

questions.prompt: Which action is safest?
choices: Share sharp tools by tossing them carefully.
questions.reviewNote: Tools should be used as instructed.

questions.prompt: What is the best response to a spill?
choices: Tell the teacher and follow directions.
Cover it with paper and leave it.
Ignore it if no one saw it.
questions.correctAnswer: Tell the teacher and follow directions.
questions.reviewNote: Spills can affect safety and the investigation.

questions.prompt: Which question helps judge reliability?
choices: Who made this source and what evidence does it use?
Does this source have the brightest colours?
Is this source the shortest one?
questions.correctAnswer: Who made this source and what evidence does it use?
questions.reviewNote: Source creator and evidence matter.

questions.prompt: Giving credit to sources is important because it...
choices: shows honesty and helps others check the information
means the student cannot explain the idea
questions.correctAnswer: shows honesty and helps others check the information
questions.reviewNote: Credit is an honest research habit.

questions.prompt: A source can be unreliable even if it has a professional-looking design.
questions.reviewNote: Appearance does not prove accuracy.

questions.prompt: Safe science includes asking for help when instructions are unclear.
questions.reviewNote: Asking for help can prevent unsafe choices.

questions.prompt: Which teamwork habit best supports STEM work?
choices: Choose the loudest person's idea every time.
questions.reviewNote: Respectful evidence-based discussion helps teams.

lessons.title: Unit 1 Final Quiz
lessons.teacherSummary: The Unit 1 final quiz checks careful observation, testable questions, fair tests, measurement, evidence, design process, safety, and research habits.
lessons.teacherOverview: Use this quiz after students complete the STEM Skills, Safety, and Investigation Habits lessons.

quiz.title: STEM Skills, Safety, and Investigation Habits Unit Quiz

questions.prompt: Which statement is the clearest observation?
choices: The liquid is light blue and has bubbles near the top.
The liquid is probably a cleaner.
The liquid wants to turn into foam.
questions.correctAnswer: The liquid is light blue and has bubbles near the top.
questions.reviewNote: A clear observation describes what is noticed.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an inference?
choices: The soil may be dry because it feels crumbly.
The soil is in a cup.
questions.correctAnswer: The soil may be dry because it feels crumbly.
questions.reviewNote: An inference is an idea based on observations.

questions.prompt: Which question is easiest to investigate fairly?
choices: Which material keeps water cold longest?
questions.correctAnswer: Which material keeps water cold longest?
questions.reviewNote: Temperature over time can be measured.

questions.prompt: Which version best improves 'Which glue is best?'
choices: Which glue holds two craft sticks together under the most weight?
Which glue has the best bottle?
Which glue do students like more?
questions.correctAnswer: Which glue holds two craft sticks together under the most weight?
questions.reviewNote: This version defines what best means.

questions.prompt: A student tests whether the amount of water affects plant height. What is the changed variable?
questions.reviewNote: The changed variable is what is changed on purpose.

questions.prompt: In the same plant test, what is the measured variable?
questions.reviewNote: The measured variable is the result.

questions.prompt: Which should stay the same in a fair plant test?
choices: The amount of water if that is being tested
questions.reviewNote: The type of plant should be controlled.

questions.prompt: Which tool measures time?
questions.reviewNote: A stopwatch measures time.

questions.prompt: Which unit could record the mass of a small object?
questions.reviewNote: Grams measure mass.

questions.prompt: A data table should be labelled clearly enough that another person can understand it.
questions.reviewNote: Clear labels make data useful.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion is most careful?
choices: The data suggests the taller ramp made the car travel farther.
The tall ramp is always the best ramp in the world.
The short ramp never works for anything.
questions.correctAnswer: The data suggests the taller ramp made the car travel farther.
questions.reviewNote: Careful conclusions avoid overclaiming.

questions.prompt: Which could be a source of error?
choices: Reading the ruler from the wrong end
questions.correctAnswer: Reading the ruler from the wrong end
questions.reviewNote: A ruler mistake can affect data.

questions.prompt: In a design challenge, criteria tell the team...
choices: what the design must do to succeed
questions.correctAnswer: what the design must do to succeed
questions.reviewNote: Criteria describe success.

questions.prompt: Which is a constraint?
questions.reviewNote: A constraint is a limit.

questions.prompt: A prototype is a model that can be tested and improved.
questions.reviewNote: Prototypes are made for testing and improvement.

questions.prompt: What should a student do if instructions are unclear?
choices: Ask the teacher or trusted adult before continuing.
Skip safety steps to save time.
questions.correctAnswer: Ask the teacher or trusted adult before continuing.
questions.reviewNote: Asking for help is a safe habit.

questions.prompt: Which question helps check a source?
choices: Who made it, and what evidence does it use?
Is it the first result online?
Does it have the biggest picture?
questions.correctAnswer: Who made it, and what evidence does it use?
questions.reviewNote: Source creator and evidence help judge reliability.

questions.prompt: Students should record what actually happened, even if the result is unexpected.
questions.reviewNote: Honest data matters in science.

unitGradePlan.note: Final STEM Skills mark recommendation: 60% unit quiz and 40% average of lesson quizzes.

unitAssessmentPlan.lessonQuizzes: Each STEM Skills lesson has a short scored quiz to check the lesson focus.
unitAssessmentPlan.unitTest: The unit quiz checks observation, questions, fair tests, variables, measurement, data, conclusions, design, safety, and research. Recommended weighting remains 40% lesson quizzes and 60% unit quiz.
